data.title: Berkshire Hathaway 'compelling buy' during market turmoil, stock up about 16% YTD
data.description:
Class B shares of Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.B) on Thursday were on track to post a six-day win streak for the first time since late January, while class A shares of the sprawling conglomerate (NYSE:BRK.A) were set for their sixth positive session in seven.

The performance underscored an overall positive trend in the Omaha, Neb.-based giant's stock in 2025. Its class A shares (NYSE:BRK.A) are +16% YTD, while its class B shares (NYSE:BRK.B) are +15.9%. That is a significant outperformance to the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500), which is -3.51% YTD. 

With Wall Street roiled by U.S. President Donald Trump's trade war this year, Berkshire's (BRK.A)(BRK.B) standing as a safe, steady stock to hold has increased.

"(The company's) record cash reserves and recent strong performance make it a compelling buy, especially during market turmoil," James Foord, investing group leader of The Pragmatic Investor, said on Monday.

"Investors should buy the dip rather than sell, aligning with Buffett's strategy of capitalizing on lower asset prices," he added, referring to Berkshire's (BRK.A)(BRK.B) legendary top boss.

The conglomerate in late February reported operating earnings of nearly $50B in 2024, and said that its cash pile had swelled to a record $334.20B.

"Despite what some commentators currently view as an extraordinary cash position at Berkshire, the great majority of your money remains in equities. That preference won’t change," Warren Buffett said in the February annual letter.

The company made headlines last year for its sizable stake reductions in Apple (AAPL) and Bank of America (BAC).

As of 1427 ET Thursday, class A shares (BRK.A) of Berkshire were +0.3% at $792,200, while class B shares (BRK.B) were +0.6% at $528.15.

data.title: Titan Machinery targets $100M inventory reduction in FY2026 amid challenging demand
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Titan Machinery (TITN) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Bryan Knutson highlighted a significant $304 million sequential inventory reduction in Q4, bringing the total fiscal year reduction to $419 million. The company now plans to shift focus from general inventory reductions to optimizing the inventory mix and addressing slower-turning equipment categories.
  • The domestic agriculture segment experienced subdued equipment demand but saw 8.2% same-store sales growth for the year, driven by service business expansion. Efforts included increased service technician headcount.
  • European operations faced challenges from drought conditions in Romania, but government subventions and potential improved weather conditions could lead to modest revenue growth in FY2026.
  • CFO Bo Larsen reported total Q4 revenue at $759.9 million, reflecting a 12% same-store sales decrease, partially offset by contributions from the Scott Supply acquisition.
  • Larsen stated, "Adjusted net loss for Q4 was $44.9 million or $1.98 per diluted share, including $0.29 benefits from manufacturer incentive plans."

Outlook

  • FY2026 guidance includes a 20%-25% revenue decline for the domestic agriculture segment. Equipment margin is expected to improve slightly to 7.7%, up from 6.7% in FY2025.
  • Additional inventory reduction of $100 million is targeted in FY2026, focusing on aging equipment and right-sizing inventory mix.
  • The construction segment is projected to decline 5%-10% in FY2026, while the European segment expects flat to 5% growth supported by stabilization in key markets like Romania.
  • Parts and service revenue is expected to achieve flat to modest growth across segments.
  • Adjusted EPS guidance for FY2026 is projected as a loss between $1.25 and $2 per diluted share.

Financial Results

  • Q4 gross profit was $51 million, a sharp decline from $141 million in the prior year, attributed to lower equipment margins due to inventory reduction measures.
  • Agriculture segment revenue declined 13.8% to $534.7 million, reflecting a 15.5% same-store sales decrease. Pre-tax loss in the segment was $56.3 million compared to $28.8 million pre-tax income a year prior.
  • Construction segment revenue was relatively flat, with a pre-tax loss of $1.7 million compared to $4.6 million pre-tax income in Q4 FY2024.
  • European segment saw a 6.1% revenue increase to $65.4 million, driven by same-store sales growth, despite a pre-tax loss of $1.8 million.
  • Australian segment revenue declined to $65.3 million, with a pre-tax income of $2.3 million, down from $4.1 million in the prior year.

Q&A

  • Ted Jackson, Northland Securities: Questioned inventory reduction methods. CEO Knutson explained most units were sold through the company's dealer network, preserving future parts and service opportunities.
  • Mig Dobre, Baird: Asked about government assistance impact on equipment demand. Knutson noted that while government payments could bolster farmer operations, equipment purchases remain closely tied to commodity prices.
  • Ben Klieve, Lake Street Capital Markets: Sought clarification on inventory targets. CFO Larsen indicated that achieving 2.5x inventory turns remains a long-term goal, with FY2026 reductions focusing on optimizing inventory mix.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concerns over continued margin pressures and the impact of challenging market conditions on inventory management and demand.
  • Management maintained a cautiously optimistic tone, emphasizing progress in inventory optimization and long-term strategic positioning.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management appeared more focused on addressing immediate challenges, while analysts remained skeptical about near-term recovery prospects.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 saw accelerated inventory reductions compared to Q3, with a $304 million sequential decrease versus $115 million in the prior quarter.
  • Management's tone shifted to a more detailed focus on inventory mix optimization and addressing slower-turning equipment.
  • Analysts continued to focus on inventory management and margin pressures, with recurring concerns about the impact of declining demand on financial performance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Persisting lower demand for large agriculture equipment, with industry volumes projected to decline 30% in FY2026.
  • Headwinds from tariff policies and uncertainty in government assistance timing and magnitude.
  • Potential further margin compression from aggressive inventory reduction strategies.

Final Takeaway

Titan Machinery's Q4 2025 earnings highlighted significant progress in inventory reduction, setting the stage for inventory mix optimization in FY2026. However, the company faces persistent industry demand challenges, with guidance reflecting continued margin pressures and modest revenue growth in select segments. Management remains focused on long-term operational efficiency and leveraging service business expansion to navigate challenging market conditions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Trump administration set to extend Chevron's Venezuela deadline by 30 days - Bloomberg
data.description:
The Trump administration is poised to extend Chevron's (NYSE:CVX) deadline to halt its Venezuela operations for at least another 30 days, Bloomberg reported Thursday.

CEO Mike Wirth asked for a possible extension during a meeting Wednesday with President Trump and other oil executives at the White House, where Trump expressed openness to the idea, according to the report.

U.S. officials have indicated to Chevron (NYSE:CVX) that it will get more time to wrap up its operations in Venezuela beyond a 30-day deadline that expires April 3, but have not worked out specifics on how much more time the company will have, the report said.

The Wall Street Journal previously reported Wirth's efforts to persuade the Trump administration to grant more time had been met with skepticism from officials who are longtime critics of Venezuela's President Maduro.

Chevron's (NYSE:CVX) operations in Venezuela account for around a quarter of the company’s total oil production and a third of its oil exports.

data.title: General Motors and Hyundai look to deepen their partnership amid geopolitical tension
data.description:
Hyundai Motor (OTCPK:HYMTF) and General Motors (NYSE:GM) are nearing a deal where Hyundai (OTCPK:HYMTF) will share two electric commercial van models with the Detroit automaker, according to Reuters. In exchange, GM (NYSE:GM) may provide Hyundai (OTCPK:HYMTF) with pickup trucks to sell under its brand in North America. The discussions are driven by rising competition from Chinese EV makers and the threat of a global trade war, prompting both companies to explore cost-saving measures through shared products.

Hyundai (OTCPK:HYMTF) plans to produce vans for both its own and GM's brands, initially importing them from South Korea but considering North American production by 2028. The talks on pickups involve GM's mid-sized trucks, though Hyundai is also interested in GM's full-sized models, which GM has not yet agreed to share. Additionally, Hyundai might supply GM with compact SUVs for the Brazilian market. The partnership aims to help both automakers navigate challenges in the global auto market, particularly the rise of Chinese EV producers and geopolitical tensions affecting trade.

General Motors (GM) and Hyundai Motor (OTCPK:HYMTF) initiated discussions last year for a strategic partnership. In September 2024, they signed a memorandum of understanding to explore collaboration on vehicles, supply chains, and clean-energy technologies. The deal marked Hyundai's (OTCPK:HYMTF) first major partnership of that nature, while GM (GM) has a history of collaborations, including past deals with Honda (HMC) and others. The partnership aims to leverage both companies' strengths to reduce costs and enhance competitiveness in key markets. 

Looking ahead, the potential partnership could expand into broader collaborations, including joint purchasing or development of computing chips, next-generation batteries, and battery materials.

data.title: U.S. boosts bird flu response with another $100M in funds
data.description:
The U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) on Thursday announced a $100M investment to combat the transmission of the bird flu virus in poultry, adding to the Trump administration's previously announced $1B initiative to fight the outbreak.

For-profit entities, including vaccine and drugmakers, will be eligible to receive the latest funds, earmarked for projects aiming to develop bird flu vaccines and therapies, Reuters reported, citing the USDA.

The U.S. bird flu vaccine stockpile includes products from GSK (NYSE:GSK), Sanofi (NASDAQ:SNY), and CSL (OTCQX:CSLLY). Vaccine developers targeting bird flu include Moderna (NASDAQ:MRNA), Pfizer (NYSE:PFE), and CureVac (CVAC). Veterinary product maker Zoetis (ZTS) offers a bird flu vaccine with a conditional license from the USDA.

Meanwhile, Agriculture Secretary Brooke Rollins added that egg imports from South Korea are also expected to begin as the Trump administration looks to address soaring prices of the kitchen staple.

In February, Turkey agreed to send more eggs to the U.S., where there is an ongoing shortage amid the death of nearly 170M egg-laying hens and other birds due to the virus. Shortly thereafter, the Trump administration announced $1B in funding to boost egg imports and prevent viral transmission.

data.title: Affirm resumed at BMO with Outperform rating on expectation of GMV/revenue upside
data.description:
BMO Capital Markets resumed coverage of buy now, pay later lender Affirm Holdings (NASDAQ:AFRM) with an Outperform rating and a $69 price target, implying a 45% price increase in the next year.

Affirm's stock has slid by more than 40% from recent highs as the probability of a recession increases and investors digest the expected impact from Klarna's OnePay deal to provide installment loans for Walmart customers.

"We expect gross merchandise value/revenue less transaction costs ("RLTC") to surprise positively (despite modest impact from Klarna-OnePay); potential credit headwinds already anticipated," analyst Rufus Hone wrote in a note to clients.

BMO's forecasts assume a mini credit cycle in FY2026, but foresees a path to ~$4.00 adjusted EPS by FY2027 in a normalized credit environment.

Affirm's (NASDAQ:AFRM) partnership with Apple (AAPL) Pay could drive low single-digit billions of incremental GMV for AFRM as early as FY2026, Hone noted.

"We expect AFRM to benefit from medium-term GMV/RLTC upside; expectations appear to contemplate a steep deceleration, despite accelerating D2C (direct-to-consumer) growth, strong momentum supporting Affirm at Checkout, and RLTC tailwinds from lower interest rates,"  the analyst said.

Furthermore, BNPL is experiencing global secular growth, driven by demographic tailwinds; i.e., younger consumers prefer BNPL financing.

BMO's Outperform rating on Affirm (NASDAQ:AFRM) contrasts with the SA Quant rating and the average SA Analyst rating, both at Hold, and aligns with the average Wall Street rating of Buy.

Affirm (AFRM) stock rose 0.8% in Thursday afternoon trading.
